steps.title: Meet the Parabola
steps.goal: See how the focus and directrix define the curve.
steps.info: Satellite dishes and car headlights are parabolic — their shape gathers incoming signals (or focuses outgoing light) at a single point called the focus.
steps.introText: A parabola is defined as the curve formed by the set of all points equidistant from some fixed point (the focus) and some fixed line (the directrix). Changing either one reshapes the curve.
steps.prompt: Drag the focus, the directrix, or the point on the curve. Notice how wherever the point sits, its distance to the focus (d₁) always equals its distance to the directrix (d₂).

steps.title: The Equation of a Parabola
steps.goal: Understand the equation of a parabola whose vertex is at the origin.
steps.introText: When its vertex is at the origin, an upward-opening parabola follows the general formula **x² = 4py**. Here, the parameter p is the distance from the vertex to the focus.
steps.prompt: Try out at least three different values of p below. Watch how each value reshapes the parabola and changes its equation.

steps.title: Match the Equation
steps.goal: Based on what you just learned, graph a parabola with equation x² = 14y

feedback.correct: Perfect! Now lets see what happens when the vertex doesn't have to be at the origin.
feedback.incorrect: The equation isn't quite there yet. Remember that 4p is the coefficient on y, so here 4p = 14. Then, what should p be?
feedback.hint: In our equation, 4p = 14 so p = 3.5. This means you should place the focus 3.5 units above the vertex.

steps.title: Vertex Form
steps.goal: Shift the parabola by placing the vertex at a non-origin point.
steps.info: When the vertex isn't necessarily fixed at the origin, the general form for the equation of a parabola is **(x − h)² = 4p(y − k)**. We still have the same paramater p, but we have two new parameters h and k. The point (h, k) gives us the vertex of the parabola.
steps.prompt: Place the vertex at (−2, 3) by moving the focus and directrix. While doing so, pay attention to how the equation of the parabola changes.

feedback.correct: Nice! The vertex is at (−2, 3). Note how the equation reflects this by including (x + 2)² and (y − 3).
feedback.incorrect: The vertex isn't at (−2, 3) yet — adjust the focus and directrix until it is!
feedback.hint: See if you can match the curve provided above. As extra guidance, you can do so by moving the focus to (-2, 5), then the directrix to y = 1.

steps.title: Graph the Equation
steps.goal: Now that you've gone over what each part of a parabolic equation means, see if you can graph a parabola just by its equation.

feedback.correct: Nice! Great job extracting key information from the equation.
feedback.incorrect: Not yet: remember that (x − 2) and (y − 1) means the vertex is at (2, 1), then 4p = 8 tells us that p should be 2.
feedback.hint: Try and match the curve provided above. As extra guidance, you can do so by moving the focus to (2, 3), then the directrix to y = -1.

steps.title: Downward Opening
steps.goal: Understand how the equation of a parabola changes if it opens downward.
steps.info: If the value of p is negative, then the direction of the parabola flips and it opens downwards. The position of the vertex is unaffected, it is essentially like anchoring the curve by the vertex and flipping it upside down.
steps.prompt: Graph a parabola with p = -2 and vertex at the origin.

feedback.correct: Excellent! p = 2 below the vertex gives x² = −8y.
feedback.incorrect: Not quite — remember, the focus should be below the vertex for a downward opening parabola. Otherwise, it is very similar to an upward opening parabola.
feedback.hint: Try and follow the curve shown above. Place the focus at (0, −2), then the directrix should be above it at y = 2.

steps.title: Shifted Narrow Parabola
steps.goal: Understand how to vary the width of a parabola.
steps.info: The width of a parabola is determined by the value of p. The larger the value of p, the wider the parabola. The smaller the value of p, the narrower the parabola.
steps.prompt: Create a narrow upward-opening parabola with vertex at (−1, 2). We will consider any parabola with p ≤ 1.25 to be narrow.

feedback.correct: Well done! A small p keeps the curve narrow even when the vertex is shifted.
feedback.incorrect: Adjust the focus and directrix until the vertex is at (−1, 2) with a small p.
feedback.hint: Try and match the curve. Move the focus to (-1, 3), and set the directrix to y = 1.

steps.title: Check Your Understanding
steps.prompt: Which statement is always true for a standard parabola?

choices.label: The vertex is at the origin.
The focus and directrix are equidistant from every point on the curve.
p equals the coefficient in front of y.

steps.feedback: As we learned, the focus–directrix distance property defines the parabola.
steps.incorrectFeedback: Not quite. Try and recall what we first introduced as the definition of a parabola.

steps.title: Mastery Check
steps.goal: Construct four parabolas with specific width, direction, and position — no hints.

parabolaSequence.label: Wide downward-opening parabola at the origin
Narrow upward-opening parabola at the origin

steps.completionMessage: Congratulations! You now understand the basics of parabolas — including shifted and downward-opening cases. Next, we'll look at circles and how they are defined.
